row.question: We're a non-resident company and Razorpay just told us that certain account terms in Part B Part I §8 only apply to us if we hold a specific type of account. What is that account type, and what regulatory documents govern whether those terms apply to us?
row.answer: The terms in §8 apply exclusively if your company holds a Special Non-Resident Rupee Account (SNRR Account). The two regulatory documents that govern this are the RBI 'Master Direction - Deposits and Accounts' dated January 1, 2016, and the Circular on 'Non-resident Rupee Accounts – Review of Policy' dated November 22, 2019. If you do not hold an SNRR Account under those directions, the §8 terms do not apply to you.

row.question: What additional charges can Razorpay apply on top of the base fees stated in my agreement?
row.persona: A merchant reviewing their payment aggregator agreement who wants to understand the full scope of potential fee-related charges beyond the headline rate.
row.clarifying_question: Could you clarify whether your situation involves the **charge_trigger_type** — specifically, are you asking about extra charges arising from (a) statutory tax changes applied to regular fees, or (b) a late/overdue payment scenario? The answer differs significantly: for tax-related additions, the clause explains that taxes are excluded from fees and any statutory variations are borne by you; for late payment, a specific interest rate kicks in on the overdue amount.

answer_branches.answer: Under your agreement, all fees quoted by Razorpay PA are exclusive of applicable taxes. Razorpay PA will charge applicable taxes on top of those fees from time to time. Importantly, if there is any statutory variation in those applicable taxes during the life of the agreement — for example, a government-mandated increase in GST — that additional cost is entirely borne by you as the Merchant. You cannot dispute or seek reimbursement for such incremental tax amounts.

clause_citations.verbatim_excerpt: Fees are exclusive of applicable taxes and Razorpay PA will charge such applicable taxes on the fees from time to time. You agree that any statutory variations in applicable taxes during the subsistence of these Terms shall be borne by You.

answer_branches.answer: If you have an overdue amount under the agreement, Razorpay is entitled to charge late payment interest at the rate of 15% per annum on that due amount. This interest accrues on the outstanding balance and represents an additional financial exposure beyond the base fees, so timely settlement of any amounts owed is important to avoid this charge.

clause_citations.verbatim_excerpt: Charge a late payment interest at the rate of 15% per annum on the due amount

Fees are exclusive of applicable taxes and Razorpay PA will charge such applicable taxes on the fees from time to time. You agree that any statutory variations in applicable taxes during the subsistence of these Terms shall be borne by You.

This is a separate and additional charge from any tax obligations under the routine billing clause.

row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business data and my customers' data for analytics and marketing. Does this comply with India's data protection laws, and what limits — if any — does RBI or DPDP Act place on how far this license actually extends?
row.persona: E-commerce merchant handling personal data of thousands of end-customers
row.answer:
This is a genuinely unsettled area, and the ToS itself does not resolve it cleanly.

**What the ToS does say:** Part A §1.2 grants Razorpay a broad, royalty-free, irrevocable, transferable, and sub-licensable license to use 'Your data, Your customer's data, information, content, trademarks, logos and any other materials/information You upload or make available.' The clause permits use for operating and improving the platform, for Razorpay's own marketing and promotional materials (without requiring further consent from you), and for analytics — with Razorpay retaining ownership of aggregated and anonymised results. The clause does add the qualifier 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.'

**What the ToS does not say:** The clause does not define what 'Applicable Laws' means in this context, nor does it enumerate which specific regulatory frameworks govern the scope of this license. Critically, it does not:
- Specify how the license interacts with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act), which imposes consent and purpose-limitation obligations on 'Data Fiduciaries' — a role you may occupy with respect to your customers' data;
- Clarify whether your customers' personal data (as distinct from your own business data) can lawfully be sub-licensed to third-party service providers under RBI's guidelines on outsourcing and data localisation (e.g., RBI's 2021 Master Direction on Payment Aggregators and Payment Gateways);
- Define what 'aggregated and anonymised' means operationally, or whether the anonymisation standard meets any regulatory threshold;
- Address whether your grant of this license on behalf of your customers is legally sufficient, or whether Razorpay must independently obtain consent from end-customers under the DPDP Act;
- State whether the 'irrevocable' nature of the license survives account termination and, if so, for how long — which may conflict with data-erasure rights under emerging Indian data protection law.

**Why this matters for you:** As the merchant, you may be a Data Fiduciary under the DPDP Act with independent obligations to your customers. Granting a broad downstream license without understanding its regulatory limits could expose you to regulatory liability that the indemnity clause in §1.2 would not protect you from — in fact, that indemnity runs *in Razorpay's favour*, not yours.

**Recommended escalation paths:**
1. **Razorpay Support / Legal Team** — Request a written clarification on how §1.2 is scoped against DPDP Act obligations and RBI PA/PG guidelines, and whether Razorpay acts as a Data Processor or co-Fiduciary with respect to customer data.
2. **Independent Legal Counsel** — Engage a privacy or fintech lawyer to assess whether your acceptance of §1.2 is compatible with your own DPDP Act obligations before going live.
3. **RBI / MeitY Guidance** — Monitor RBI's evolving circulars on Payment Aggregators and MeitY's rules under the DPDP Act (rules not yet fully notified as of mid-2025) for authoritative boundary-setting on data use by payment intermediaries.

No confident resolution can be given here — the interaction between this contractual license and India's layered regulatory framework remains genuinely unclear.

clause_citations.verbatim_excerpt: You grant a royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable, transferable and sub-licensable license to Razorpay PA, the Group Entities and third-party service providers, to use Your data, Your customer's data, information, content, trademarks, logos and any other materials/information You upload or make available to us or on the Platform… You agree that Razorpay PA may use Your materials in its marketing and promotional materials without requiring any incremental consent from You… Razorpay PA shall retain ownership of the results or reports derived from such data (which shall be in aggregated and anonymised form) for its business purposes in accordance with Applicable Laws.

retrieval_trace.silence_hint: The ToS defers to external regulation (RBI / NPCI / state law) without defining boundaries.

row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business data and customer data for analytics and retain ownership of derived reports. But how far does this go under RBI's data-localisation and data-privacy rules? Can Razorpay share those derived reports with overseas group entities or third-party sub-licensees without my explicit consent?
row.persona: A mid-sized e-commerce merchant concerned about data sovereignty and compliance with Indian data-protection regulations
row.answer:
This is a genuinely uncertain area where the ToS provides a partial answer but leaves critical boundaries undefined.

**What the ToS does say (Part A §1.2):** Razorpay is granted a broad, royalty-free, irrevocable, transferable, and sub-licensable licence over your data and your customers' data. It may use those materials for analytics, retain ownership of derived aggregated and anonymised reports, share them with 'Group Entities and third-party service providers', and use them in marketing — all without requiring incremental consent from you. The clause does add a limiting phrase: 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.'

**What the ToS does not say:** The clause does not define what 'Applicable Laws' means in this context, nor does it specify which laws govern cross-border transfers of derived data. It does not:
- Identify whether RBI's Payment Aggregator / Payment Gateway (PA/PG) Guidelines (March 2020 and subsequent circulars) restrict how payment-related customer data may be shared with overseas entities or sub-licensees;
- Clarify whether the data-localisation requirements under RBI's Storage of Payment System Data directions (April 2018) apply to derived/aggregated reports, or only to raw transaction data;
- Address how the forthcoming Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA) obligations interact with this broad licence — particularly around the lawful basis for processing customer personal data and any cross-border transfer restrictions;
- Define 'anonymised' to a legal standard (e.g., whether it meets the irreversibility threshold that regulators would require before data falls outside personal-data protection rules);
- Specify whether the sub-licences granted to overseas Group Entities or third-party service providers are subject to any contractual data-processing safeguards, standard contractual clauses, or RBI approval.

**Why this creates genuine ambiguity:** The clause explicitly defers to 'Applicable Laws' without defining their scope or hierarchy. RBI guidelines on payment data localisation, NPCI operational circulars, and the DPDPA each impose distinct obligations that may constrain — or even conflict with — the broad licence Razorpay has taken. Whether a derived, aggregated report constitutes 'payment system data' under RBI rules, or 'personal data' under the DPDPA, is not answered here and may depend on the specific data points included.

**Recommended escalation path:**
1. **Razorpay Support / Data Protection Officer:** Request Razorpay's written clarification on how it interprets 'Applicable Laws' in §1.2, which specific RBI/NPCI circulars it considers binding on derived-data sharing, and whether a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) is available.
2. **Independent Legal Counsel:** Engage a lawyer specialising in Indian fintech and data-privacy law to assess your exposure under the DPDPA, RBI PA/PG Guidelines, and RBI's data-localisation circular before onboarding sensitive customer data.
3. **RBI / NPCI Guidance:** For authoritative interpretation of whether aggregated/anonymised payment-derived reports are subject to localisation or transfer restrictions, consider seeking clarification directly from RBI's Department of Payment and Settlement Systems or reviewing NPCI's published operational guidelines.

In short, the clause establishes a very wide licence in Razorpay's favour, but the regulatory boundaries of that licence — particularly for cross-border data flows and sub-licensing — remain undefined in the ToS itself and will depend on external regulatory interpretation.

row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business's data and my customers' data for analytics and marketing without asking me again. Does this comply with India's data protection laws, and where exactly does Razorpay's licence to use my customers' personal data end?
row.persona: E-commerce merchant concerned about customer data privacy and regulatory compliance
row.answer:
This is a genuinely unsettled area where the ToS and the broader regulatory landscape leave important questions unanswered.

**What the ToS does say (Part A §1.2):** Razorpay's terms grant it a 'royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable, transferable and sub-licensable license' to use both your data and your customers' data for operating and improving the platform, for Razorpay's own marketing and promotional materials, and for analytics — with Razorpay retaining ownership of aggregated and anonymised results. The clause states this use will be 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.'

**What the ToS does not say:** Several critical boundaries are left undefined:
- The clause does not specify which laws constitute 'Applicable Laws' for data use purposes — it does not name the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA), RBI's data localisation circulars, or any other specific statute.
- It does not explain what consent mechanism, if any, Razorpay relies upon when processing your *customers'* personal data — your customers have no direct contractual relationship with Razorpay, yet their data is included in the licence grant.
- It does not define the boundary between 'aggregated and anonymised' analytics outputs (which Razorpay owns) and identifiable customer data, leaving open the risk of re-identification.
- It does not clarify whether the 'irrevocable' nature of the licence survives termination of your merchant account, or how that interacts with data deletion rights under emerging Indian privacy law.
- The marketing use provision — which requires no 'incremental consent' from you — does not address whether your customers' consent is separately required under applicable privacy frameworks.

**Why this is genuinely ambiguous:** The phrase 'in accordance with Applicable Laws' defers the entire question of permissible scope to an external regulatory framework that is itself in flux. India's DPDPA 2023 has not yet been fully operationalised, RBI's payment aggregator guidelines impose separate data storage and localisation obligations, and NPCI rules may apply to specific payment rails. The interaction between these frameworks and the broad licence grant in §1.2 is not resolved within the ToS itself.

**Recommended escalation steps:**
1. **Razorpay Support / Data Protection Officer:** Ask Razorpay in writing to clarify the legal basis for processing your customers' personal data under §1.2, and to confirm what 'Applicable Laws' they rely upon.
2. **Legal counsel:** Engage a privacy or fintech lawyer to assess whether the licence grant in §1.2 is compatible with your own obligations under the DPDPA 2023 as a 'Data Fiduciary' with respect to your customers.
3. **RBI / MeitY guidance:** Monitor RBI's Payment Aggregator guidelines and MeitY's rules under the DPDPA for any specific requirements that would constrain or override contractual data-sharing arrangements like this one.

No confident resolution can be given here — the answer depends on regulatory developments and interpretations that are still evolving.

You agree that Razorpay PA may use Your materials in its marketing and promotional materials without requiring any incremental consent from You. row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business's customer data and materials for analytics and retain ownership of the derived reports. Does this comply with RBI's data localisation and data-sharing rules, and what limits — if any — apply to how far Razorpay can share that data with its 'Group Entities and third-party service providers'?
row.persona: E-commerce merchant processing significant transaction volumes who is concerned about customer PII flowing to unknown third parties and about RBI data-localisation obligations
row.answer:
This is a genuinely unsettled area, and a confident answer cannot be given based on the Razorpay ToS alone.

**What the ToS does say (Part A §1.2):** Razorpay grants itself a royalty-free, irrevocable, transferable, and sub-licensable licence over 'Your data, Your customer's data, information, content, trademarks, logos and any other materials/information You upload or make available.' It states that derived analytics reports will be in 'aggregated and anonymised form' and used 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.' The clause names Group Entities and third-party service providers as permitted recipients but does not enumerate who those parties are or where they are located.

**What the ToS does not resolve:**
1. *Scope of 'Applicable Laws'*: The clause defers entirely to external regulation without defining which laws govern, which version of those laws controls, or how conflicts between jurisdictions are resolved. The RBI's Payment Aggregator & Payment Gateway Guidelines (March 2020 and subsequent circulars), the RBI's data-localisation directions for payment system operators, and the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA) all potentially apply — but the ToS is silent on how §1.2 aligns with each of these frameworks.
2. *Identity and location of third-party service providers*: The ToS does not list or categorise the third parties who may receive your customers' data, making it impossible to assess cross-border transfer risk under RBI localisation norms or DPDPA Chapter IV provisions.
3. *Meaning of 'anonymised'*: The ToS asserts reports will be 'aggregated and anonymised' but provides no standard or methodology. Whether this meets the anonymisation threshold under DPDPA or RBI guidance is not addressed.
4. *Merchant's own obligations to its customers*: The indemnity in §1.2 runs in Razorpay's favour, but the ToS does not clarify whether the merchant is expected to obtain separate consent from its end-customers for this downstream data use — a question that may be answered differently under DPDPA consent requirements versus older IT Act/Rules frameworks.
5. *Revocability of the licence*: The licence is described as 'irrevocable,' yet DPDPA gives data principals the right to withdraw consent. The interaction between these two positions is unaddressed.

**Recommended escalation paths:**
- **Razorpay Support / Data Protection Officer**: Request a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) or Data Sharing Addendum that explicitly names sub-processors, specifies data-residency commitments, and maps §1.2 to RBI and DPDPA obligations.
- **Legal counsel specialising in Indian fintech/data-privacy law**: Obtain an opinion on whether your merchant agreement, read together with §1.2, satisfies your obligations to your own customers under DPDPA and RBI PA-PG Guidelines before processing live transactions.
- **RBI / NPCI guidance**: Consult the RBI's published circulars on data localisation for payment system participants and, if relevant, NPCI's operating guidelines, to determine whether the sub-licensing chain described in §1.2 is permissible for the specific categories of payment data you handle.

In summary, §1.2 is broad, the external regulatory landscape is evolving, and the ToS itself acknowledges external law controls — without defining what that means in practice. This warrants escalation before assuming compliance.

row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business data, customer data, and logos in its marketing materials without asking me again. But my customers' data is also subject to India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act and RBI's data-localisation guidelines. Does Razorpay's licence grant override those external rules, or do I still need separate customer consent before their data can be used in Razorpay's promotional activities?
row.persona: A mid-size e-commerce merchant onboarding to Razorpay Payment Aggregator, who has a dedicated data-privacy officer and is concerned about regulatory compliance under DPDPA 2023 and RBI guidelines.
row.answer:
This sits in a genuine regulatory grey zone, and no confident resolution can be offered here.

**What the ToS does say (Part A §1.2):** Razorpay's terms grant the company a 'royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable, transferable and sub-licensable license' to use 'Your data, Your customer's data, information, content, trademarks, logos and any other materials/information You upload or make available.' The clause explicitly permits use of these materials 'in its marketing and promotional materials without requiring any incremental consent from You.' It also states that analytics results will be 'in aggregated and anonymised form' and that use will be 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.'

**What the ToS does not say:** The clause defers to 'Applicable Laws' without defining which laws govern which categories of data, or how conflicts between the contractual licence and those laws are resolved. Specifically, the ToS:
- Does not define whether 'Your customer's data' used in marketing is limited to aggregated/anonymised form or can include identifiable data;
- Does not specify how the licence interacts with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA), which requires a lawful basis and, in many cases, explicit consent from the data principal (your customer) for processing their personal data for marketing purposes;
- Does not address RBI's Payment Aggregator/Payment Gateway guidelines or data-localisation norms, which impose restrictions on how payment-related customer data may be stored, shared, and used;
- Does not clarify whether your acceptance of these terms constitutes a valid 'consent mechanism' that you can pass on to your customers, or whether you must independently obtain customer consent;
- Does not describe what happens if a customer exercises a DPDPA right (e.g., withdrawal of consent or erasure) that conflicts with the irrevocable nature of the licence grant.

**Why this matters for you:** If customer personal data is used in Razorpay's promotional materials — even in aggregated form — and that use is later found to lack a valid legal basis under DPDPA or to breach RBI's guidelines, the indemnity clause in §1.2 could expose you (the merchant) to claims, since you warranted that Razorpay's use of Your Materials is lawful.

**Recommended escalation paths:**
1. **Razorpay Merchant Support / Data Protection team** — Request written clarification on exactly what categories of customer data are used in marketing, whether it is always anonymised, and how Razorpay's use aligns with DPDPA obligations.
2. **Legal counsel** specialising in Indian data-protection and fintech regulation — Assess whether your existing customer consent flows (privacy notice, terms of purchase) adequately cover downstream use by Razorpay as a data processor or joint controller.
3. **RBI / NPCI guidance** — Review the latest RBI PA/PG guidelines and any NPCI circulars on data-sharing to determine whether payment-related customer data is subject to restrictions that would limit the scope of §1.2 regardless of contractual agreement.
4. **DPDPA compliance review** — Consult the Data Protection Board (once operational) or seek a legal opinion on whether the irrevocable licence structure is compatible with a data principal's statutory right to withdraw consent.

row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business data and my customers' data for analytics and marketing. Does this comply with India's data protection laws, and what limits — if any — does RBI or NPCI place on how Razorpay can share or sub-license that data to third parties?
row.persona: E-commerce merchant processing customer payments through Razorpay, concerned about data privacy obligations under Indian law
row.answer:
Part A §1.2 of Razorpay's ToS grants Razorpay PA a 'royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable, transferable and sub-licensable license' to use your data, your customers' data, trademarks, logos, and other materials for operating the platform, marketing, and analytics. It also states that aggregated and anonymised analytics results are owned by Razorpay, and that this use occurs 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.'

What the ToS does not define is which specific 'Applicable Laws' govern this data use, or how those laws constrain the breadth of the license. Several significant gaps remain unresolved:

1. **RBI and NPCI frameworks**: The RBI's Payment Aggregator and Payment Gateway guidelines (March 2020 and subsequent circulars) impose obligations on payment aggregators regarding data storage, localisation, and permissible use of transaction data. The ToS defers to these frameworks by referencing 'Applicable Laws' but does not specify which RBI/NPCI rules apply, how they limit the sub-licensing right, or what happens when a third-party sub-licensee's use conflicts with those rules.

2. **Customer data and consent**: The clause covers not just your business data but also your *customers'* data. Whether Razorpay's use of end-customer data for marketing purposes is permissible — and whether your acceptance of these terms is sufficient consent on behalf of your customers — is not addressed. India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA) introduces consent and purpose-limitation requirements that may interact with this broad license grant in ways the ToS does not clarify.

3. **Scope of 'anonymised and aggregated'**: The ToS states analytics results will be in 'aggregated and anonymised form,' but does not define the standard of anonymisation, leaving open whether it meets the threshold under DPDPA or any sector-specific RBI standard.

4. **Irrevocability and your right to withdraw**: The license is described as irrevocable, but data protection frameworks typically preserve a data principal's right to withdraw consent or request erasure. The ToS does not address how these rights interact with the irrevocable license.

Given these unresolved questions, it is not possible to say with confidence whether the license grant as written fully complies with all applicable Indian data protection and payment regulation, or precisely where RBI/NPCI rules limit Razorpay's or its sub-licensees' use of your and your customers' data.

**Recommended escalation steps:**
- Contact **Razorpay's merchant support and Data Protection Officer (DPO)** to request a written clarification of which specific RBI circulars and data-protection frameworks govern §1.2, and how sub-licensee use is restricted.
- Consult **independent legal counsel** with expertise in Indian fintech regulation and the DPDPA to assess whether your acceptance of this clause creates liability toward your own customers.
- Review **RBI's Payment Aggregator guidelines** (RBI/2019-20/193 and updates) and any **NPCI operational circulars** directly to understand the regulatory floor that 'Applicable Laws' is meant to incorporate.

You agree that Razorpay PA may use Your materials to operate and improve the Platform and/or the Services and fulfil Razorpay PA's rights and discharge its obligations under the Terms, including providing the Services. row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business data and my customers' data for analytics and marketing. Does this comply with India's data protection laws, and what limits does RBI or DPDP Act place on how far Razorpay can go with that license?
row.persona: E-commerce merchant handling significant volumes of customer personal data (names, contact details, payment history)
row.answer:
This is a genuinely uncertain area, and a confident answer cannot be given here.

**What the ToS does say:** Part A §1.2 grants Razorpay PA a 'royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable, transferable and sub-licensable license' to use 'Your data, Your customer's data, information, content, trademarks, logos and any other materials/information You upload or make available.' The clause permits use for operating and improving the Platform, for Razorpay's 'marketing and promotional materials without requiring any incremental consent from You,' and for analytics — with Razorpay retaining ownership of 'results or reports derived from such data (which shall be in aggregated and anonymised form)' in accordance with 'Applicable Laws.'

**What the ToS does not say:** The clause defers compliance to 'Applicable Laws' without defining which laws govern, what those laws require, or how Razorpay interprets its obligations under them. Specifically, the ToS is silent on:
- Whether the license grant is consistent with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act), which imposes obligations on 'Data Fiduciaries' and 'Data Processors' regarding purpose limitation, consent, and cross-border transfers.
- What RBI's guidelines on data localisation (PA/PG Guidelines, 2020) and storage of payment data permit or prohibit in the context of analytics and marketing use.
- Whether your customers' personal data can be included in marketing use under §1.2, or whether that use is restricted to aggregated/anonymised forms only — the clause is ambiguous on this boundary.
- What 'aggregated and anonymised' means by Razorpay's own definition, and whether that standard meets the DPDP Act's or RBI's threshold for anonymisation.
- Whether the 'irrevocable' and 'sub-licensable' nature of the license survives regulatory changes or a merchant's termination of services.
- What indemnity obligations you may face if a regulator finds that your grant of this license to Razorpay itself violated your customers' data rights.

**Why this matters for you as a merchant:** You are granting a broad, irrevocable license over your customers' personal data. If that grant is later found to be non-compliant with the DPDP Act or RBI directions, the indemnity clause in §1.2 could expose you — not Razorpay — to liability for claims arising from Razorpay's use of that data.

**Recommended escalation paths:**
1. **Razorpay Support / Account Manager** — Request a written clarification of exactly what 'Applicable Laws' means in §1.2, and what categories of customer data are used for marketing versus analytics-only purposes.
2. **Legal Counsel** — Engage a lawyer specialising in Indian data protection and fintech regulation to assess whether your acceptance of §1.2 creates compliance exposure under the DPDP Act, 2023, and RBI's Payment Aggregator Guidelines.
3. **RBI / MeitY Guidance** — Monitor published guidance from the Reserve Bank of India on PA/PG data obligations and from the Ministry of Electronics & Information Technology on DPDP Act implementation rules, as these will define the outer boundaries of what this license can lawfully cover.

row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business's customer data and materials for analytics and marketing. But what are the actual limits on how long it can retain that data, and which specific RBI or data-protection regulations govern what it can do with my customers' personal information?
row.persona: E-commerce merchant processing high volumes of customer transactions
row.answer:
This is a genuinely uncertain area where the Terms of Service provide only a partial answer, and important boundaries remain undefined.

**What the ToS does say:** Part A §1.2 grants Razorpay PA a 'royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable, transferable and sub-licensable license' to use your materials — including your customers' data — to operate and improve the Platform, for marketing and promotional purposes, and for analytics. It further states that analytics results will be retained by Razorpay PA in 'aggregated and anonymised form' and that all of this is to be conducted 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.'

**What the ToS does not say:** The clause defers entirely to 'Applicable Laws' without specifying which laws apply, what retention periods are permissible, what anonymisation standards must be met before data is considered outside the scope of personal data protection, or what rights your customers have to opt out or request deletion. It does not define the boundary between permissible 'aggregated and anonymised' analytics and processing that would require explicit customer consent under India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA) or any sector-specific RBI guidelines on data localisation and storage (e.g., RBI's Storage of Payment System Data circular). It also does not clarify whether the 'irrevocable' nature of the licence conflicts with data-subject deletion rights under applicable privacy law, nor does it address NPCI rules that may separately govern how payment-related customer data can be used for secondary purposes like marketing.

**Why this matters:** The interaction between a broad, irrevocable contractual licence and statutory data-subject rights (including the right to erasure) is an unresolved tension that the ToS language alone cannot settle. The phrase 'in accordance with Applicable Laws' is a deferral, not a definition.

**Recommended escalation steps:**
1. **Razorpay Support / Data Protection Officer:** Request Razorpay's data processing addendum or privacy notice that operationalises §1.2, including specific retention schedules and anonymisation standards they apply.
2. **Legal Counsel:** Engage a privacy lawyer familiar with the DPDPA 2023 and RBI payment data regulations to assess whether the irrevocable licence in §1.2 is enforceable to the extent it conflicts with statutory data-subject rights, and what disclosures you owe your own customers.
3. **RBI / NPCI Guidance:** Review RBI's circular on Storage of Payment System Data and any NPCI operating guidelines applicable to your payment instruments to understand the regulatory floor that 'Applicable Laws' is meant to incorporate.

No confident resolution can be offered here — the answer depends on regulatory interpretations that the ToS expressly leaves to external law without defining the boundaries.

row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my customer data for analytics and retain ownership of aggregated/anonymised reports. Does this comply with RBI's data localisation and storage norms, and can Razorpay share those derived reports with third parties outside India?
row.persona: A mid-sized e-commerce merchant concerned about customer data privacy and regulatory compliance under RBI payment aggregator guidelines.
row.answer:
This is a genuinely unsettled area where the Terms of Service provide only a partial answer, and several important questions remain open.

What the ToS does say: Part A §1.2 grants Razorpay a 'royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable, transferable and sub-licensable license' to use your data and your customers' data to operate and improve the Platform, for marketing purposes, and to conduct analytics. It further states that Razorpay 'shall retain ownership of the results or reports derived from such data (which shall be in aggregated and anonymised form) for its business purposes in accordance with Applicable Laws.' The phrase 'in accordance with Applicable Laws' is the critical qualifier — it defers compliance obligations to external regulation without defining what those laws require or how they are applied.

What the ToS does not say: The clause does not specify which external laws govern cross-border transfer of derived analytics reports — for example, whether RBI's data localisation requirements under the Payment Aggregator and Payment Gateway (PA-PG) Guidelines (March 2020 and subsequent circulars) restrict where such reports may be stored or shared. It does not define what 'aggregated and anonymised' means in practice, nor whether that standard satisfies the threshold required under applicable Indian data protection frameworks (including the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, to the extent it applies). It does not clarify whether the sub-licensable nature of the licence allows derived reports to be transferred to entities outside India, or whether the irrevocable nature of the licence survives merchant account termination in a manner consistent with RBI storage timelines. These are precisely the kinds of boundaries that RBI and NPCI circulars address externally, but which the ToS leaves undefined.

Given this genuine regulatory gap, it would be inadvisable to assume either that such sharing is permitted or that it is prohibited without further verification. Recommended escalation paths include: (1) contacting Razorpay's merchant support or Data Protection Officer in writing to request clarification on how 'Applicable Laws' is operationalised for cross-border data transfers; (2) consulting independent legal counsel with expertise in Indian fintech and data protection regulation; and (3) reviewing the RBI PA-PG Master Directions and any NPCI circulars directly, or seeking informal guidance from RBI's regulatory sandbox or fintech outreach channels if the matter is material to your business.

row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business data and my customers' data for analytics and marketing. Does this comply with India's data protection laws, and what are the actual limits on how far Razorpay can go with that data under RBI/NPCI rules?
row.persona: E-commerce merchant processing customer payments through Razorpay, concerned about data privacy obligations
row.answer:
This is a genuinely unsettled area where the ToS, applicable regulation, and emerging law do not together draw a clear boundary.

What the ToS does say: Part A §1.2 grants Razorpay a 'royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable, transferable and sub-licensable license' to use your data and your customers' data to operate the platform, for marketing and promotional materials ('without requiring any incremental consent from You'), and to conduct analytics — with Razorpay retaining ownership of aggregated and anonymised results 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.' The clause also makes you responsible for indemnifying Razorpay against claims arising from its use of your materials under this provision.

What the ToS does not say: The clause defers compliance to 'Applicable Laws' without defining which laws govern which use case. It does not specify: (a) whether the broad marketing licence is consistent with RBI's guidelines on storage and use of payment data (including the 2019 circular on storage of card data and the PA/PG Master Directions 2020); (b) how the licence interacts with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA), particularly the requirement for a lawful basis for processing customers' personal data and the limits on secondary use; (c) what 'aggregated and anonymised' means in practice and whether Razorpay's standard satisfies regulatory definitions of anonymisation; (d) whether sub-licensing your customers' data to third-party service providers requires separate consent from those customers; and (e) how state-level or sector-specific rules (e.g., healthcare, financial services) might further restrict permissible data uses.

The irrevocable and transferable nature of the licence, combined with the marketing use right granted without incremental consent, may sit in tension with data-subject rights under the DPDPA and with RBI's data localisation and purpose-limitation expectations — but whether an actual conflict exists, and how it would be resolved, is not answered within the four corners of the ToS.

Recommended escalation: (1) Contact Razorpay's merchant support and Data Protection Officer in writing to request a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) that maps each use case to a specific legal basis and identifies which third-party sub-processors receive customer data. (2) Seek advice from a qualified privacy or fintech legal counsel to assess your own obligations as a data fiduciary under the DPDPA before relying on this licence. (3) Review RBI's Master Directions on Payment Aggregators and Payment Gateways (2020, as updated) and any NPCI circulars relevant to your payment instruments, since those instruments set independent constraints that override contractual terms. Do not assume the ToS alone settles your compliance position.

row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business data, customer data, and logos in its marketing materials without asking me again. But doesn't RBI's data localisation and data-sharing rules limit what Razorpay can actually do with payment data? Where exactly does the ToS end and RBI regulation begin?
row.persona: A mid-sized e-commerce merchant concerned about regulatory compliance and data privacy obligations
row.answer:
This is a genuinely unresolved area where the ToS and external regulation overlap in ways the document itself does not fully clarify.

**What the ToS does say:** Part A §1.2 grants Razorpay PA a 'royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable, transferable and sub-licensable license' to use your data, your customers' data, trademarks, logos, and other materials for operating and improving the Platform, for marketing and promotional purposes, and for analytics — with the caveat that analytics outputs will be 'in aggregated and anonymised form' and used 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.' The clause also makes you responsible for indemnifying Razorpay against claims arising from its use of your materials under this clause.

**What the ToS does not say:** The clause phrases its limits by referencing 'Applicable Laws' without defining which laws govern which categories of data, or how conflicts between those laws are resolved. Specifically, the ToS does not:
- Define whether 'customer data' includes payment instrument details, UPI handles, or card-on-file data that may be subject to RBI's Payment Aggregator and Payment Gateway (PA-PG) guidelines or RBI's Storage of Payment System Data circular;
- Clarify whether the 'transferable and sub-licensable' scope of the licence is constrained by RBI's data localisation requirements (which mandate that certain payment data be stored only in India);
- Specify whether sharing your brand assets (logos, trademarks) in Razorpay's marketing requires separate consent under any applicable advertising standards or intellectual property law;
- Describe what 'aggregated and anonymised' means in practice, or whether that standard meets the anonymisation threshold under any forthcoming framework such as India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA).

**Why this matters:** The licence as written is broad and irrevocable. If RBI or NPCI rules impose restrictions on how payment-related customer data may be shared with third-party service providers or used in analytics, those restrictions would theoretically apply — but the ToS does not map out those boundaries, leaving merchants uncertain about their own compliance exposure, especially given the indemnity obligation they accept.

**Recommended escalation:**
1. **Razorpay Support / Account Manager** — Ask for a written clarification of which categories of data fall within the §1.2 licence and how Razorpay's use of that data is scoped against RBI PA-PG guidelines.
2. **Legal Counsel** — A lawyer familiar with RBI payment system regulations, the DPDPA, and IP law should review whether the irrevocable, transferable licence conflicts with any regulatory data-handling obligations you independently carry as a merchant.
3. **RBI / NPCI Guidance** — For questions specifically about permissible use and cross-entity sharing of payment transaction data, the RBI's PA-PG Master Directions and any NPCI circulars on data governance are the authoritative external sources, but interpreting how they interact with a private contract requires professional advice.

In short, the ToS acknowledges that 'Applicable Laws' constrain the licence but does not define those boundaries — so the precise limits of what Razorpay can legally do with your data, and what liability you carry under the indemnity, remain genuinely uncertain without further clarification.

row.answer:
This is a genuinely unsettled area, and the ToS does not draw a clean line between Razorpay's contractual licence and the obligations imposed by external regulation.

**What the ToS does say (Part A §1.2):** You grant Razorpay PA a 'royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable, transferable and sub-licensable license' to use your data, your customers' data, trademarks, logos and other materials for operating and improving the Platform, for marketing and promotional purposes, and for analytics — with Razorpay retaining ownership of aggregated, anonymised results. The clause states this use will be 'in accordance with Applicable Laws,' which is the key phrase that defers to external frameworks without defining them.

**What the ToS does not say:**
- It does not specify which version or provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act 2023, RBI's Payment Aggregator & Payment Gateway guidelines, or any state-level consumer protection law take precedence when they conflict with the broad licence granted here.
- It does not clarify whether the irrevocable, sub-licensable nature of the licence survives a regulatory direction to delete or restrict use of customer personal data.
- It does not define what 'aggregated and anonymised' means in practice, nor whether that standard meets the anonymisation threshold required under DPDP Act rules or RBI's data storage norms.
- It does not address whether the blanket marketing consent you give on behalf of your customers (whose data is also licensed) is sufficient under the DPDP Act's purpose-limitation and consent requirements, or whether Razorpay separately obtains that consent from end-customers.
- It does not specify geographic or cross-border data transfer limits, leaving RBI's data localisation requirements for payment data unaddressed within the clause itself.

**Why this matters:** The phrase 'in accordance with Applicable Laws' is an external deferral — it acknowledges that regulation governs, but it does not resolve the conflict if the licence grant is broader than what those laws permit. Relying solely on this clause to assess your compliance exposure would be insufficient.

**Recommended escalation path:**
1. **Razorpay Support / Legal Team** — Request a written clarification on how §1.2 is operationalised in light of the DPDP Act and RBI PA/PG guidelines, and whether Razorpay obtains separate consent from your end-customers for use of their personal data.
2. **Your own legal counsel** — Engage a privacy or fintech lawyer to assess whether the irrevocable licence you have granted is enforceable to the extent it concerns customer personal data, and whether your own privacy notices to customers adequately disclose this downstream use.
3. **RBI / NPCI guidance** — Check RBI's Master Directions on Payment Aggregators (March 2020 and subsequent updates) and any NPCI circulars for explicit data usage and localisation requirements that may constrain what Razorpay can contractually claim.

No confident resolution can be offered here without clarity from those sources.

row.question: If I build a white-label checkout interface using Razorpay's APIs and brand it with my own logo, does the restriction on 'derivative works' and 'marks' in the ToS conflict with RBI's co-branding guidelines for payment aggregators, and which set of rules takes precedence?
row.persona: A fintech startup founder who has received RBI's payment aggregator authorisation and wants to offer a co-branded checkout experience to their merchant clients, using Razorpay as the underlying PA infrastructure.
row.answer:
This is a genuinely unresolved question and no confident answer can be offered here.

What the Razorpay ToS does say: Part A §1.1 grants you only a 'personal, revocable, non-exclusive, non-transferable, limited right to access the Platform and make use of the Platform and the Services.' The same clause explicitly prohibits you from creating a 'derivative work,' using Razorpay's marks without explicit consent, and sub-licensing or transferring any right in the Services. It also requires you not to remove or obscure any proprietary rights notices.

What the ToS does not say: The clause does not define where the boundary lies between a permissible custom integration and a prohibited 'derivative work.' It does not address how its IP restrictions interact with RBI's Master Directions on Payment Aggregators and Payment Gateways (or any co-branding circulars issued by RBI/NPCI), which may independently govern how a licensed PA may present its brand alongside an underlying infrastructure provider. The ToS contains no carve-out or safe harbour for regulatory co-branding obligations, nor does it specify which instrument — the ToS or applicable RBI/NPCI directions — controls in the event of a conflict. The phrase 'explicit consent' is used without defining the form, scope, or duration of such consent.

Because the ToS defers implicitly to external regulation without defining the boundary, a genuine regulatory gap exists. Depending on how RBI's PA Master Directions are interpreted, your co-branding arrangement might be required, permitted, or restricted — and the ToS offers no guidance on reconciling those obligations with its IP restrictions.

Recommended escalation steps:
1. Contact Razorpay's merchant support and legal/partnerships team directly to request written clarification on what co-branding or white-label use cases are covered under 'explicit consent' and whether a separate IP licence or addendum is available.
2. Engage independent legal counsel with payments-regulation expertise to analyse whether your proposed checkout interface constitutes a 'derivative work' under Indian IP law and how RBI's PA Master Directions interact with the ToS restrictions.
3. Seek guidance from RBI's Department of Payment and Settlement Systems and review NPCI circulars on co-branding to understand any mandatory disclosure or branding obligations that may apply to your licence.

Do not proceed with the co-branded deployment until written clarity is obtained from Razorpay and a legal review is complete.

clause_citations.verbatim_excerpt: Razorpay PA grants You a personal, revocable, non-exclusive, non-transferable, limited right to access the Platform and make use of the Platform and the Services in accordance with the Terms. You shall not download, copy, create a derivative work, modify, reverse engineer, reverse assemble, transmit or otherwise attempt to discover any source code, sell, assign, sub-license, grant a security interest in or otherwise transfer any right in the Services or marks. You do not have the right to use any of our marks without explicit consent from us.

ambiguity.what_is_known: Part A §1.1 prohibits creating derivative works, using Razorpay's marks without explicit consent, and sub-licensing or transferring any rights in the Services. It grants only a limited, personal, non-transferable licence to access the Platform.
ambiguity.what_is_missing: The ToS does not define the boundary between a permissible custom integration and a prohibited derivative work, does not address how its IP restrictions interact with RBI's PA Master Directions or NPCI co-branding rules, provides no conflict-resolution mechanism specifying which instrument prevails when regulatory obligations and ToS restrictions diverge, and does not define the form or scope of 'explicit consent' for mark usage.

retrieval_trace.silence_hint: The ToS defers to external regulation (RBI / NPCI / state law) without defining boundaries.
